problem_owner_org.name: Lakeside CNC Group
problem_owner_org.description: US-based FANUC CNC repair and maintenance specialist.

problem.title: Needs to replace spreadsheet-based CNC repair intake with a scalable service workflow
problem.description: Lakeside CNC Group outgrew spreadsheets and basic email-based tracking as its repair-management workload increased. The case study says customer questions and service requests were still being recorded in spreadsheets, which limited visibility and made it harder to manage a growing technical repair operation.

requirements.body: Centralize incoming repair requests and customer communications in one system instead of spreadsheets and inboxes.

citations.evidence_note: Supports the growth from a two-person repair shop, spreadsheet-based request handling, and the need to redesign repair management and improve data visibility.

requirements.body: Improve visibility into open jobs, request status, and internal follow-up responsibility.

citations.evidence_note: Supports the growth from a two-person repair shop, spreadsheet-based request handling, and the need to redesign repair management and improve data visibility.

requirements.body: Support a growing repair workload without relying on ad hoc manual processes.

citations.evidence_note: Supports the growth from a two-person repair shop, spreadsheet-based request handling, and the need to redesign repair management and improve data visibility.

requirements.body: Preserve a customer-service process that can respond consistently as operational complexity increases.

citations.evidence_note: Supports the growth from a two-person repair shop, spreadsheet-based request handling, and the need to redesign repair management and improve data visibility.

pilot_framework.scope: Pilot the new workflow on incoming repair inquiries, ticket intake, technician assignment, and customer status updates for the FANUC repair operation.
pilot_framework.suggested_kpis: Track time to log a new request, percentage of requests handled without spreadsheet use, average response time to customer inquiries, and visibility of open repair jobs.
pilot_framework.success_criteria: The pilot succeeds if intake and follow-up are consistently handled in the centralized workflow, staff can see current repair status without spreadsheet lookups, and customer communications are easier to track.
pilot_framework.common_pitfalls: Common pitfalls include keeping parallel spreadsheet processes alive, incomplete technician adoption, and failing to define consistent status updates for customer communication.
pilot_framework.duration: A 6- to 8-week pilot is appropriate for one service workflow covering request intake through repair-status communication.
pilot_framework.resource_commitment: Requires a service lead, one or two technicians or coordinators, and limited admin support to define statuses, migrate active work, and review workflow adoption.

citations.evidence_note: Supports the growth from a two-person repair shop, spreadsheet-based request handling, and the need to redesign repair management and improve data visibility.
